name: lint-and-validate
description: Automatic quality control, linting, and static analysis procedures. Use after every code modification to ensure syntax correctness and project standards. Triggers onKeywords: lint, format, check, validate, types, static analysis.
capabilities: Detects project type (Node.js/TypeScript or Python) automatically
Runs ESLint with --fix flag for Node.js projects
Executes TypeScript compiler type checking with tsc --noEmit
Runs Ruff linter and Bandit security scanner for Python projects
Executes MyPy for Python type hint validation
Runs npm audit for Node.js dependency security checks
author: sickn33
securitySummary: Static analyzer flagged 20 patterns but all are false positives. The subprocess.run calls execute known linter commands derived from project configuration, not user input. Markdown documentation examples were incorrectly flagged as shell execution. No cryptographic code exists. The skill is safe for publication with documented external command usage.
valueStatement: Code modifications often introduce syntax errors or violate project standards. This skill automates linting, type checking, and security audits to catch issues before commit.
userTitle: Run linting and validation on your code
limitations: Requires linter configuration files to exist in project root
Cannot fix complex type errors automatically
Limited to 120 second timeout per linter execution
Does not support languages beyond Node.js/TypeScript and Python

useCases.title: Pre-commit validation for developers
useCases.description: Run comprehensive linting before committing code to ensure all changes meet project standards.

useCases.title: Code review preparation
useCases.description: Validate code changes before submitting pull requests to reduce review cycles.

useCases.title: Legacy code modernization
useCases.description: Identify type coverage gaps and linting issues when migrating older codebases.

promptTemplates.title: Basic lint check
promptTemplates.prompt: Run the lint and validate skill on my current project to check for any issues.
promptTemplates.scenario: Quick validation after making code changes

promptTemplates.title: Targeted path validation
promptTemplates.prompt: Lint and validate all files in the src/components directory and fix any auto-fixable issues.
promptTemplates.scenario: Focused validation on specific code areas

promptTemplates.title: Full project audit
promptTemplates.prompt: Run complete validation including lint, type check, and security audit. Generate a summary report of all findings categorized by severity.

promptTemplates.title: Type coverage analysis
promptTemplates.prompt: Analyze type coverage across the entire codebase. Identify files with less than 70 percent type coverage and list untyped functions that need annotations.

bestPractices: Run linting after every code modification, not just before commits
Configure linter rules in project root files before running validation
Fix type errors immediately rather than accumulating technical debt
antiPatterns: Skipping validation to meet deadlines introduces bugs in production
Ignoring type checker errors leads to runtime failures
Running linters without --fix flag misses automatic corrections

faq.question: What happens if my project has no linter configured?
faq.answer: The skill detects missing configuration and suggests creating .eslintrc, tsconfig.json, or pyproject.toml files with appropriate settings.

faq.question: Can this skill fix all linting errors automatically?
faq.answer: No. The --fix flag handles auto-fixable issues like formatting. Logic errors, type mismatches, and complex violations require manual fixes.

faq.question: Does this work with monorepos?
faq.answer: Yes. Run the skill with a specific project path argument to validate individual packages within a monorepo structure.

faq.question: How long does validation take?
faq.answer: Typical runs complete in 5 to 30 seconds. Each linter has a 120 second timeout for large codebases.

faq.question: What languages are supported?
faq.answer: Node.js, TypeScript, and Python. Other languages require custom linter integration.

faq.question: Is my code sent to external services?
faq.answer: No. All linting runs locally using installed tools. No code is transmitted externally.
